questions.question: Explain the FCFS disk scheduling algorithm with an example. Why is it rarely used in practice?
keyPoints: FCFS services requests in order of arrival
Example: if requests come for tracks 50, 100, 30 - service in same order
Simple to implement - no complex calculations
High average seek time
No starvation but poor performance under load

questions.question: What is seek time and rotational latency? How do they affect disk performance?
keyPoints: Seek time: time for head to move to correct track
Rotational latency: time for sector to rotate to head position
Seek time is typically larger component
Both contribute to disk access time
Minimizing these improves overall system performance

questions.question: Differentiate between SCAN and C-SCAN algorithms. Which one provides uniform wait times?
keyPoints: SCAN: services in both directions, reverses at ends
C-SCAN: services in one direction only, returns empty
SCAN has variable wait times based on position
C-SCAN provides uniform wait times
C-SCAN more predictable for real-time systems

questions.question: What is starvation in disk scheduling? Which algorithms can suffer from starvation?
keyPoints: Starvation: when some requests never get serviced
SSTF can starve distant requests
SCAN/LLOOK avoid starvation by servicing all requests in path
FCFS/C-SCAN are starvation-free
Fairness vs efficiency tradeoff in algorithms

questions.question: Explain the LOOK algorithm. How does it differ from SCAN?
keyPoints: LOOK is a variant of SCAN algorithm
Head only moves to last request in each direction
SCAN goes to end of disk regardless of requests
LOOK is more efficient - no unnecessary movement
Similar performance benefits as SCAN

questions.question: Calculate the total head movement using FCFS and SSTF for the following: Head starts at 50, Requests at: 82, 170, 43, 94, 25, 110
keyPoints: SSTF significantly reduces seek time
Shows importance of good scheduling

questions.question: Write the formula for total disk access time and explain its components.
keyPoints: Disk Access Time = Seek Time + Rotational Latency + Transfer Time
Seek Time: head movement to correct track
Rotational Latency: average half rotation waiting time
Transfer Time: time to read/write data
Access time depends on disk physical characteristics

questions.question: With a disk having 200 tracks (0-199), the read/write head is currently at track 50 and the request queue contains: 95, 180, 34, 119, 12, 190. Calculate the total head movement for FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, and C-SCAN algorithms. Which algorithm gives minimum seek time?
keyPoints: SSTF gives minimum seek time but may cause starvation
SCAN balances performance and fairness

questions.question: Explain the SCAN disk scheduling algorithm in detail. What are its advantages and disadvantages? Compare it with C-SCAN.
keyPoints: SCAN works like an elevator - services requests as head moves in one direction
Head moves from one end to other, servicing all requests in path
Then reverses direction and repeats
Advantages: good seek time performance, no starvation
Disadvantages: may have unequal wait times, goes to end even without requests
vs C-SCAN: C-SCAN returns empty providing uniform wait times
C-SCAN more suitable for systems requiring fairness
SCAN better for general purpose systems

questions.question: Discuss the SSTF algorithm. Why is it more efficient than FCFS but can cause starvation? How can starvation be prevented?
keyPoints: SSTF selects request closest to current head position
Minimizes average seek time compared to FCFS
Simple to implement - just find nearest request
Problem: distant requests may never be serviced if new closer requests keep arriving
This is called starvation or convoy effect
Prevention: Use SCAN or LOOK algorithms
These service all requests in path, preventing indefinite waiting
Modern OS use combinations or hybrid approaches
Tradeoff between efficiency and fairness

questions.question: Compare all disk scheduling algorithms (FCFS, SSTF, SCAN, C-SCAN, LOOK, C-LOOK) based on: (a) Average seek time (b) Starvation (c) Fairness (d) Implementation complexity
keyPoints: (a) Avg Seek Time: SSTF < SCAN/LOOK < C-SCAN/C-LOOK < FCFS
(b) Starvation: SSTF can starve; FCFS/SCAN/LOOK/C-SCAN/C-LOOK are starvation-free
(c) Fairness: FCFS most fair; C-SCAN uniform wait; SSTF least fair
(d) Complexity: FCFS simplest (O(n)); others need sorting O(n log n)
FCFS: Simple, fair, poor performance
SSTF: Good performance, starvation risk
SCAN: Good balance, may have unequal waits
C-SCAN: Uniform waits, slightly more movement
LOOK: Efficient SCAN variant
C-LOOK: Best of C-SCAN without unnecessary movement
